candidates.stmt:
I’m a lifelong Battersea resident, born and raised in Wandsworth, and proud to call this vibrant, diverse community my home. I went to primary school here, and like many local families, I’ve seen both the challenges and opportunities that shape our borough.

I worked as a primary school teacher for nearly thirty years, teaching across all key stages and holding senior leadership roles in state schools across London. My focus has always been on inclusion, raising standards, and ensuring every child, whatever their background, has the chance to succeed.

I’ve built my career on listening, understanding and finding practical solutions. I have learnt how to work with families, schools and local services to get things done. I believe in empathy, collaboration and delivering real results.

I’m standing to be a councillor in Battersea Park ward because I want to give back to the community that shaped me. I want to help build a fairer Wandsworth, where people can continue to access safe, affordable housing, rely on high-quality local services and have real opportunities to thrive.

I’m passionate about education, the arts, community investment and greener neighbourhoods especially the gem that is Battersea Park itself; my own green space and ‘garden’ as a child. I understand the variety and range of pressures people face in our ward and I will always be ready to listen, support and offer solutions.

I bring experience, energy and determination and I’ll be a strong, visible and active voice for our community, working tirelessly to deliver the best possible outcomes for the residents of Battersea Park ward.

Born and raised in London, Ben lives with his partner In the Surrey Lane estate. He works in renewables and is committed to cutting bills by championing insulation and local community initiatives. Ben believes together we can beat the cost of living crisis and build a better future.

Antony has been resident in Battersea Park since 2014 and has offices in the Power Station. A former Olympic coach and British Army Captain he has been building global tech companies in London for over 30 years as a serial entrepreneur and as a financier.

Battersea is the only place he wants to live and he is a big fan of the Park and local amenities. He lives with his partner in Battersea and has daughter who is now a doctor.

Jasmin grew up in Putney, so has always had a strong connection to the local area.

Jasmin works in education policy in her day job, volunteers as a school governor for West Hill Primary School and is a Suicide Prevention Champion for West Central London Mind.

She currently lives near East Putney station with her flatmates and is committed to working with residents to make the area she grew up the best it can be.

Dermot lives in and grew up in the ward, and has been a Governor of St Joseph’s RC Primary School on Oakhill Road since 2018.

Dermot is a solicitor at a City law firm, specialising in Employment law. He previously worked as a civil servant working on matters including Brexit and the response to the Covid pandemic.

Dermot is committed to making our brilliant corner of London even better, for all its residents!

I am passionate about a shift to bold, truthful & compassionate politics that puts quality of life for everyone as the top priority. Where change isn't dismissed as "too hard" & where I'm empowered to make the world a better place for my children.

I moved here 20 years ago to study at St George's University, and it has been my home ever since. I have built my life here, and I care deeply about making sure local people feel heard, respected and properly represented.

I co-chair my NHS staff disability network and am a longstanding trade union representative. Through these roles I am involved in writing and shaping policy to make sure people are represented fairly and their voices are properly heard. I actively seek input from staff throughout the organisation to ensure everybody feels valued and included. Much of my work involves supporting carers and families who are too often left to fight for things they should not have to battle alone, whether that is accessibility, support for children, adaptations, social care or fair treatment at work.

At the same time, I stand for the wider community too, including people who feel isolated, and those who can easily become unseen when support is hard to reach. I want East Putney to be a place where people from all backgrounds and all walks of life know their concerns are valid and genuinely do matter. In the disability community we have a saying 'nothing about us without us', and when this is applied to everyone, equality and diversity stop being slogans and start becoming culture. I want the residents of East Putney to feel a sense of belonging and that this truly is their home.

One of the things I have particularly enjoyed while canvassing has been the good conversations with people whose politics differ from my own. Even when we disagree, those conversations have value. They reinforce that local politics works best when people are willing to listen and learn from one another and bring different perspectives to the table. We are a politically diverse borough, where even individuals in the same household support different parties. I am proud to live in a democratic country, but it's unfortunate there is an ugly side to politics where negative stereotypes are perpetua

I've spent nearly three decades building things with communities, not for them. As a researcher and educator, I understand green isn't just environmental policy, it's about **bringing life to Battersea, and bringing Battersea to life**. When we embed green technologies, we _create the spaces_ where neighbours meet, create, and belong. We foster genuine friendships. We **build** neighbourhoods where families thrive. We **grow** resilience.

I've mentored hundreds of people from a simple principle: _listen first_, then act on what you hear. Access to digital skills, cultural space, and genuine community ownership transform what's possible.

For Falconbrook, I will work to:

- Protect and expand the spaces where neighbours create and connect
- Champion digital equity and skills, not as charity, but as community asset
- Build green infrastructure that strengthens our neighbourhood bonds and resilience
- Ensure residents have a real voice in decisions that shape our ward

Falconbrook deserves a councillor who _listens_ harder than they _speak_.

I care about the cost of living, decent housing, safe streets and properly funded local services.

Decisions are made about us without us, instead, we need decisions about us, made by us.

I’m a working-class candidate who’s had enough of a system that takes us for granted. While politicians treat us as votes, I’ve been on estates, in workplaces, and alongside residents. I believe in people power, I’m standing to give a voice to the voiceless and challenge a political class that has abandoned us. Battersea deserves more than promises, it deserves real power.

Born and raised in Furzedown, Eden has lived, studied and worked in the area her entire life. With a degree in Sociology, Eden is dedicated to justice across social, environmental and economic causes, and aims to improve the standard of living in Furzedown by championing diversity and investing in the community.

Adam is a lifelong South Londoner and has lived with his wife and twin sons in Furzedown since 2021. He works for a Black-led community organisation as an activist researcher, and is Trustee of a youth foundation.

He is determined to advocate for marginalised and minoritised voices. Passionate about health, environmental and racial injustice, and a champion of the creative arts, he is committed to bringing both seriousness and sparkle to local government.

Diane has lived in Furzedown for over 30 years and is a teacher and a National Education Union (NEU) officer.She believes in putting people and the environment before profit. Diane is passionate about working for a fairer society, including access to properly funded education and affordable housing for all.

I am proud to be the Conservative parliamentary candidate for Stockport at the 2017 general election.

We need an election now to secure the strong and stable leadership the country needs to see us through Brexit and beyond. The choice is between strong and stable leadership in the national interest with Theresa May and the Conservatives – or weak unstable coalition government led by Jeremy Corbyn.

I have had strong links with Stockport my entire life – my family run an electronics business based in the borough that does business all around the world, my grandfather taught at Manchester University and grandmother worked in clinical support at Stepping Hill and Wythenshawe Hospitals.

Like so many people locally, my family rely on local NHS services.  As your MP, I will work tirelessly to expose Labour  mismanagement on Stockport Council and fight for the high quality services people in Stockport deserve.

As a former local councillor, I have experience of leading campaigns to regenerate town centre areas that suffer from empty retail units and a lack of investment and helping local residents resolve problems with local and national government bodies.

Professionally, I advise businesses in a range of sectors – from leisure to manufacturing – on how to get the best deal from government. I have worked with businesses to attract millions of pounds of investment to the UK.

I will use this experience on the campaign trail to make the case for continued investment in local jobs and infrastructure.

We need  to negotiate the best deal for Britain in leaving the EU and to forge a new role for ourselves in the world.  Brexit means Brexit and we’re going to make a success of it.

If you elect me as your Member of Parliament, I will hit the ground running to deliver positive change for Stockport.  Please do not hesitate to get in touch with me if I can help in any way.

Source: http://www.danhamilton.co.uk/

I am Michael DiStefano, Green Party, candidate for nine elms. With almost 2 decades experience as a sports agent, I've also lived here for around 10 years – through its rapid development – and seen firsthand the impact on residents. I'm standing to push for affordable living, fairer systems, including district heating questions, and the a community built for people – not profit, fully supporting the Green party manifesto.

Aled has served as a Northcote councillor since 2018. He has lived locally for nearly 10 years and loves our part of Wandsworth. He is a Governor of Ark Bolingbroke Academy, a member of the Friends of Wandsworth Common (and avid litter-picker), and an advisor to the Clapham Junction Business Improvement District which supports our local businesses in Northcote and Clapham Junction.    

During the covid pandemic, Aled held the Council’s portfolio with responsibility for businesses and the local economy in Wandsworth, and oversaw payments of business grants to local businesses affected by covid and led the successful implementation of the Northcote Road summer pedestrianisation scheme.    

Beyond the Council, Aled works in central London as a barrister specialising in commercial and intellectual property law. In his spare time, he enjoys live music, cosy pubs, and belgian beer (ideally all at the same time).

I'm a father of two and I moved to Battersea six years ago. What I want more than anything is for my children to be able to grow old on a peaceful, liveable planet. Neither Keir Starmer or Rishi Sunak will deliver that. That's why I’m running in the general election for the Green Party.  

We need a World War II-sized mobilisation to change our entire society. Here in Battersea we should be preparing for floods, extreme heat, extreme storms and food shortages. 

Of course I would welcome your vote but that’s not enough - especially in a seat like Battersea which is projected to be safe Labour this time around.

Our politicians have failed in their most fundamental duty - to keep us and our children safe. 

Our political system has failed to deal with this crisis.  

So what now?

I'm urging all Battersea residents to get involved in a new Battersea Popular Assembly. We, the people of Battersea, need to come together, get to know each other, organise and prepare for the challenges ahead. I support the Green Party’s manifesto, but if elected, I will also work to bring about the decisions of the assembly.

Born in Portsmouth and brought up in the ambience of the Royal Navy, educated at a local Grammar School and focussed on Physics, Chemistry and Biology. At 16 he flew gliders and single propeller aircraft solo in the RAF Scholarship he gained at 18. He gained his commission as Pilot Officer in the RAF and flew the Jet Provost solo and the Vickers Varsity and the HS125 Dominie jet as navigator. The Defence cost cuts finished his career early in less than three years.

A period at IBM was followed by Reading University for a science degree in Psychology, Physiology & Biochemistry and Zoology – graduating with a good B.Sc. Vacation work paid the rent but also introduced Stephen to ICL, the UK computer company he joined post graduation and where he trained as a technical author, and then as copy writer in ICL’s Head Office in Putney. He joined the National Computing Centre gaining sales experience of software, education & standards. In 1974 he joined BIS in Waterloo where he He established the sales team, the marketing team and, thanks to his psychology knowledge, grew the Aptitude Testing activity to a significant revenue with UK sales and his European clients.

In 1989 Stephen created two Putney-based companies, TTI providing experienced IT trainers for the Spanish market and another providing consulting, training and documentation: CHL. As an English & French speaking consultant and documentation specialist he produced high quality web sites (HBS for FIFA), documentation, training courses and videos on time and on budget. He developed and / or used documentation standards for complex financial and technology organisations and training staff in using many software tools in ten European countries.

He produced clear and concise calls for tender, proposals, reports, business plans, procedures, help systems and websites for international clients in finance, telecommunications & IT, broadcasting, tourism and in European administration.

Now retired, he has given much time

Roy has been a member of the Green Party, and stood as an election candidate in Wandsworth for more than 40 years.  He is a botanist and writer who devotes much time to encouraging others to share his enthusiasm for the natural world.  His current interests include reducing light pollution, building strong local communities, and the provision of an efficient National Health Service for all.  More long term interests include greater provision of facilities for young people, lifestyles, decentralisation, the provision of more public conveniences, and encouraging active lifestyles.

John Pindar has lived in Wandsworth and Lambeth since 1984. He was a Liberal Democrat councillor in Lambeth for eight years. He had a career in the teaching profession, teaching in various types of schools, and lecturing at two FE Colleges, in the subjects of Geography and Politics. He is now a full time novelist and also undertakes research into various eastern and south-eastern European countries.

My name is Charlie Robinson. I am a father of three daughters, a local businessman, and a long-term Wandsworth resident who cares deeply about the future of our borough.

I am standing because too many residents feel they are paying more and getting less in return. Our streets should feel safer, cleaner, and better managed. Residents deserve a council that focuses on core services, financial discipline, and visible results.

My priorities are simple:

* Tackling anti-social behaviour and restoring a visible local presence that helps residents feel safe in their communities.
* Cleaner streets, faster removal of fly-tipping and graffiti, and higher standards across public spaces.
* Greater financial accountability and transparency so residents know where their money is going.
* Better value for taxpayers by focusing council spending on frontline services and practical outcomes.
* Listening to residents properly and acting on local concerns rather than ignoring them.

Wandsworth should be a borough that families, businesses, and communities are proud to call home. I believe local government should work for residents first, deliver efficiently, and be accountable for its decisions.

I want to help restore trust, standards, and pride in our borough.

Sue has lived on the Grid in Southfields with her husband for 17 years. Originally from South Africa, Sue was active in the anti-apartheid movement and later worked on the international campaign against landmines — a career spent fighting injustice. That commitment continues today.

Sue works for a leading legal charity and is a trustee of a local advice charity supporting people across Wandsworth. She is one of the co-founders of Recycle More Southfields and has been a passionate advocate for our neighbourhood for nearly two decades.

Kester grew up, went to school and lives in Southfields. He will fight for sustainable growth and development across the ward - ensuring both local businesses and local green spaces get the support they need. Kester is a strong advocate of social justice and believes we need a national conversation to ensure, in a time of instability and rising rents and mortgages, everyone can afford a decent standard of living.

candidates.n: Caroline India Gwendoline De la Soujeole

candidates.stmt: A Wandsworthian since 1989, Michael wants a campaigning council, challenging this terrible Government and fighting for funding for essential services; publicly declaring support for the people of Gaza, and disinvesting in companies which invest in Israel. He also wants the Council to prioritise services and facilities for the young and to reduce street crime by offering hope and opportunity.

Nicola lives in Thamesfield and is trained as an accountant. She wants to work with our community to help make it stronger, fairer and more resilient for the future. She believes we need to address climate, health and inequality together in order to tackle both the cost of living and climate crises and improve all our lives. Her priorities are to rapidly roll out insulation, solar panels and heat pumps in our homes, reduce pollution and safeguard our green spaces.

As a mother of two little boys, Yannina is appalled by the state of things in England and abroad. She understands that in a world that is interconnected, global security and national security go hand in hand. Her priority as councillor would be to empower local communities and build climate resilience whilst tackling deep inequality.

Lisa has lived locally for 27 years and works in higher education, supporting young people to reach their potential. Lisa believes more can be done to restore and strengthen services, and to build a fairer, greener future. She is committed to celebrating our diverse community while protecting public spaces and helping local businesses thrive.

I lived in Tooting Bec for nearly 10 years. I am fluent in Urdu/Hindi, Punjabi, and Pashto, with functional knowledge of Arabic and Persian. I am an advocate of the High Court and a lawyer in Pakistan, recently called to the Bar at Middle Temple as a Barrister. I support charity work as a legal caseworker or paralegal, including Bail for Immigration Detainees, handling bail applications for immigration detainees. As a candidate for Tooting Bec councillor, I strongly support more social housing, free education, free school meals, additional playgrounds and sports facilities for youth, and the promotion of local small businesses. I would like the council to invest in leisure centres, safe cycling routes, benches for adults and pregnant women, and increased CCTV for safety, along with strict measures against shoplifting and violence targeting children and women. If elected, my priorities include fostering integration, promoting diversity, and ensuring a safe and happy community.

I have now been your local Councillor for four years and in that time I have achieved lots for our Town, for example, helping secure £15m in funding for a new leisure Centre. I have lived in Bedworth all of my life, I attended Nicholas Chamberlaine and I now work as a Parliamentary Researcher. 

You get value for money under me. I have not claimed any expenses since 2018. I have held 68 surgeries since my election and my Full Council attendance is 97%. And, as residents will know, I always respond to calls no matter the time of day. 

I am incredibly proud of what Bedworth has achieved in the last year. £15m secured for the Town, a new BMX track in the park, a new leisure centre on the horizon, the Market back outside, cheaper parking charges, and a review of Labour's broken Borough Plan. We have to stick with a Conservative Council at NBBC after May, don't put our successes at risk by voting Labour!

All the best,

Kyle.

I have three priorities:

1) To treat cost of living and rising rents as an emergency and look at any possible relief for residents in Tooting Broadway. I know what it's like to have essential needs be unmet by underfunded public services and a timid Labour government.

2) To raise the bar for the presence of councillors in the ward. I commit to spending one day a week door knocking locals to proactively flush out as many issues as possible, on top of the normal ward surgeries and prompt responses to emails. Your councillor should make it as easy for you as possible to get issues fixed.

3) To be a campaigning councillor. That which is not within a councillors official remit can still be within a councillor's influence. I will use my experience as an organiser and campaigner, in partnership with residents themselves, to put pressure on relevant authorities be it Transport for London, Wandsworth Council, or the Mayor of London, to have our needs met.

Glyn Goodwin is standing for election in Wandle ward on the 7th May 2026. 
As a long time **local resident** in the ward for most of his life he is well aware of local concerns. 
He pledges to **respond to emails** in a timely manner if elected as councillor, something which has been severely lacking as pointed out by many residents. 
He wants to bring in local ward-level **local democracy** where residents have their say, and with a budget attached.
Too often planning seems to happen to people rather than taking residents concerns properly. 
Wandsworth Green Party is keen to tackle the **cost-of-living crisis.** 
By attracting internal **investment** through its New Wandsworth Future Fund, it will start to benefit those worse off and bring greater **fairness** to Wandsworth.
He worked on **The Earlsfield Leveller** which redistributed funds to those most in need from those that were happy to donate their government heating funds.
https://www.earlsfieldfoodbank.org.uk/news/local-leveller-scheme-launched-in-earlsfield
The cost-of-living crisis and planning decisions are things which have affected Wandle. 
The Atheldene re-development has taken 13 years so far and is still incomplete as design changed from 43 townhouses to 196 dwellings, before expanding further.
He saw first hand as it was bulldozed through council and wants meaningful consultation for residents.  
As winner of the Mayors award for tackling climate change he is aware of how issues combine. With 73% of London trees at risk, planting the right trees in the right place is essential for our wellbeing.
He will aim to increase tree canopy cover across the ward, to help with the urban heat island effect.
He is also works with local refugee group Just Shelter, https://www.justshelter.co.uk

I am a public servant. Unlike career politicians, I have seen firsthand how neglected streets, overdevelopment, and broken council promises hurt Wandle.

We need CHANGE! and this is why I am humbly requesting that you lend me your vote.

With your help, I will fight to stop overdevelopment to protect Wandle's identity and industrial heritage, and demand infrastructure before more homes.

On safer streets: anti-social behaviour is our top reported crime. I will fight for better street lighting, safer walking routes, and visible ward officers with real powers.

On council tax: they claim " tax freeze" but your bills actually rose 2.4% last year via hidden social care levies.

I will fight for transparency and local infrastructure.

On 7 May, use your vote wisely and vote for Wil Lindalo, a councillor on your side and not more of the same

Why Vote for Me

Local people are best represented by local councillors who live in our neighbourhood and are accountable to our neighbours:

As a councillor of the Wandle Independent Group, I will:

Listen to residents and respond to concerns
Raise issues consistently until they are addressed
Put Wandle Ward first - not party politics
Work for you all year round, not just at election time

Andrew MacMillan
Wandle Independent Group

I was born and raised in Wandsworth. It’s where I call home. Our area and community have helped to shape who I am and with the world in turmoil I want to do want I can to give back and protect and enhance the place we all call home.

Who am I?
I hold an MSc in Gender, Peace and Security studies for the London School of Economics and Political Science. I’ve been a student of history, interested in why things are they way they are and how can we stop repeating the same mistakes. I have experiencing campaigning for women’s rights and peace organisations and currently works as Strategy Review Officer for a Charitable Trust which supports the social justice sector and those working to change things for the better. Central to my work and approach is the aim to address the root causes of injustice, inequality and conflict.

I take an intersectional approach, understanding that issues of climate crisis, wealth and social inequality, the cost-of-living crisis, the housing crisis, increased war and conflict are all interrelated issues that operate on a global and national scale but have very real impacts on people’s day-to-day lives. Our current system prioritises profit over people and planet, while wealth generation is necessary this system is not sustainable. It means the shareholders of the privatised water companies and fossil fuels companies are raking in billions while we have the privilege of footing the bill through higher fuel, energy and water bills and having sewage dumped in our rivers and waterways.

Increasingly it feels like our communities are being divided while the basics required to live a fulfilling life move further out of reach or are unaffordable for many. Things feel like they are getting worse, not better. This is why I am standing as a Green Party councillor candidate.

What would I do as councillor? 
If elected, I will work to improve local democracy to make sure residents are involved in decision making and budget spending; push to protect our Gre

Sacha has been active in trade union, socialist, climate and social justice struggles for many years. Previously a fire safety officer, he now works for a campaigning organisation. He will push for more council housing, stronger workers' rights and restoration of the funding for local services stripped away by central government since 2010.

Malcolm has represented West Hill Ward since 1994 and has lived in Wandsworth for nearly 40 years. He has worked on all aspects of the Council’s services, including supporting residents over the AELTC’s plans for Wimbledon Park Golf Course; pressing for tighter controls on Lime Bikes; pushing TfL to improve the West Hill subway at the top of Beaumont Road; addressing the growing problem of litter in the area; promoting the interests of Council tenants and leaseholders; and addressing many thousands of individual problems with housing, schooling, social care and so on. He has published a book looking at the history of West Hill and Wimbledon Park Side over the last 200 years or more. He works in energy policy and is often seen and heard on TV and radio.

Good  evening everyone, I’m Jason Whiffin. I’ve lived in the area for about 12 years, and up by the Common for the past 9, and I’d like to thank you tonight for this opportunity to represent the Greens here in West Hill. 

I’d like if I may to open with a weather report.  It’s going to be 43 degrees tomorrow in New Delhi. That’s 109 degrees Fahrenheit. And the day after, and the day after that. It hit 40 degrees in New Delhi on 29 March this year and the long term forecast suggests that it’s going to stay in the mid 40s for several weeks more. The Indian Meteorological Department notes that such extended heat is unusual so early in the year and links it to climate change.

Which is why I’m here.  I believe we are sleepwalking into a climate catastrophe. And when I discovered that there was no one standing for the Green Party in West Hill in the local elections, I thought well, there’s no point getting annoyed and expecting someone else to step up, so I put myself forward so that anyone who wanted to register a Green vote could be counted.

It frustrates me that we have such a democratic deficit with our first past the post system that thousands of votes are disregarded every election time because they have been cast for a so-called “minority” party.  Climate change is going to affect all of us, and we deserve to have representatives on our councils who are putting it front and centre. This democratic deficit and the lack of representation fuels polarising views. The structure also makes the process in general adversary rather than collaborative. I believe we need collaboration more than ever to solve these huge problems, and if I was elected I would want to work with my fellow councillors to find the solutions.

My background is quite mixed.   I grew up on a council estate, went to the local comprehensive, have a degree in Economics and started my career in accountancy.  I moved to IT within Finance and now work for a large Finance corporation in the City. 

Cycling

A Plea for Restoration: From One Failing State to Another?
To the residents of our community,
I didn’t come to the United Kingdom just for a change of scenery. I came here for a future. 
Having grown up in South Africa, I have seen first-hand what happens when a nation’s infrastructure crumbles, when the rule of law becomes a suggestion, and when public funds vanish into the pockets of the incompetent. I lived through that "Third World" transformation—the potholes that become craters, the utilities that fail, and the sense of unease that settles over a neighbourhood when the authorities lose control.
I moved my life across the globe because Britain represented the "First World" standard: order, safety, and accountability. But lately, I look around and feel a haunting sense of déjà-vu. I see a country in decline, and I refuse to stand by and watch the UK repeat the mistakes of the country I left behind.
Faith, Morality, and Our Foundations
As a practising Christian, my world-view is shaped by the belief that a strong society must be built on a firm foundation. British society was built upon Christianity, traditional morals, and clear values. These are the pillars that once made this nation a beacon of stability for the rest of the world.
I value and respect these heritage values deeply. If we stop upholding the moral fabric that binds us—honesty, personal responsibility, and respect for our neighbours—we lose the very essence of what makes this country home. We must return to a system that rewards hard work and protects the vulnerable, rather than one that merely manages decline.
My Pledges to You:
 * End Council Waste: I’ve seen how "political stunts" drain a city dry. We will scrap vanity projects and bloated bureaucracy. Your hard-earned money should serve your family, not a councillor’s ideological agenda.
 * Put Frontline Services First: In a First World nation, the basics are non-negotiable. We will prioritise filling potholes, reliable bin collections, and com

A vote for Reform offers a fresh approach to change and an opportunity for real improvement that benefits you.

Having lived in West Putney for many years with 2 rescue dogs, I have deep roots in our local community and I will act on your priorities, not just make empty promises.

As an IT software company director and business owner, I also served as Chairman of a Museums Trust charity. Actively raised funds for various charities and ran the London Marathon for endangered wildlife. Currently involved in environmentally friendly and nutritious meals to schools and local councils.

I will address key issues in our ward such as community safety and crime prevention, improving transportation, housing, public facilities, and supporting a successful high street. I will protect Putney's unique social environment and remove wasteful Council expenditure.
